It builds a data-driven business case with cost comparisons, which precisely matches the need for an on-premises baseline and an AWS cost projection.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes existing AWS resource utilization to recommend optimal instance types, not on-premises baselines or migration cost projections. Option B is incorrect because AWS Cost Explorer visualizes and forecasts existing AWS spend, not the cost of workloads still running on premises. Option C is incorrect because the AWS Systems Manager Agent is software installed on instances to enable management tasks; it is not a tool for baselining on-premises environments or projecting migration costs.

255.text:
Option A is correct because once the Free Tier period expires or usage exceeds the Free Tier limits, AWS simply bills the customer at the standard pay-as-you-go rates for any usage beyond the free allowances. There is no penalty or special process; the account continues operating and the customer pays only for the additional consumption at normal on-demand pricing.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Support does not contact customers to arrange charges; billing is automatic and self-service. Option C is incorrect because Free Tier usage within the limits is genuinely free, so the customer is not retroactively charged for consumption during the Free Tier period. Option D is incorrect because exceeding Free Tier limits does not freeze the account or require a payment plan; the account remains active and is charged standard rates for the overage.

256.text:
Option D is correct because the AWS Cloud Development Kit lets developers define cloud infrastructure using familiar programming languages including TypeScript, Python, Java, C#/.NET, and Go. The CDK synthesizes this code into AWS CloudFormation templates for provisioning, so the company can model and manage its infrastructure with the common programming languages it already uses.

Option A is incorrect because AWS CodeBuild is a managed build service that compiles source code and runs tests as part of CI/CD pipelines, not an infrastructure-as-code framework based on programming languages. Option B is incorrect because AWS CloudFormation provisions infrastructure using declarative JSON or YAML templates rather than general-purpose programming languages. Option C is incorrect because the AWS CLI is a command-line tool for issuing individual API commands and does not allow defining infrastructure in TypeScript, Python, Java, and .NET as the requirement specifies.

257.text:
Option A is correct because Migration Evaluator is a complimentary AWS service that gathers on-premises usage data and produces data-driven business cases for migrating to AWS. It quantifies the projected cost and savings of running workloads in the cloud, helping organizations plan and justify their migration, which exactly matches creating data-driven business cases for cloud planning.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Billing Conductor is used to customize billing and pricing for end customers in consolidated billing scenarios, not to build migration business cases. Option C is incorrect because the AWS Billing Console manages and views actual AWS account charges and payment methods, not projections for prospective cloud planning. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Forecast is a machine learning service for time-series forecasting of business metrics such as demand, not a tool for building cloud migration business cases.

258.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Infrastructure Event Management is a structured program available to Enterprise Support (and Business Support for a fee) customers that provides architectural and operational guidance to prepare for planned high-traffic events such as product launches. AWS engineers help assess scaling readiness, review the architecture, and provide real-time support during the event, directly addressing the company's need to assess readiness to scale.

Option A is incorrect because replacing EC2 instances with Lambda functions is a major re-architecture, not an assessment of scaling readiness, and may not suit the existing application. Option C is incorrect because AWS Marketplace is for purchasing third-party software and services, not for requesting AWS to monitor a launch event. Option D is incorrect because Cost Management coverage reports focus on Reserved Instance and Savings Plans coverage for cost optimization, not on evaluating whether the infrastructure can scale for a traffic surge.

259.text:
Option D is correct because On-Demand Instances let the company pay for compute capacity by the hour or second with no long-term commitment, which is ideal for short-term, experimental, or unpredictable workloads running for only 3 to 6 months. The flexibility to start and stop instances without upfront payment or term obligations makes On-Demand the appropriate pricing model for this temporary use case.

Option A is incorrect because Savings Plans require a 1-year or 3-year commitment, and a 3-year term would lock the company into far longer than the 3-to-6-month experiment. Option B is incorrect because Dedicated Hosts provide physical servers for compliance or licensing needs at higher cost, which is unnecessary for experimental workloads. Option C is incorrect because Reserved Instances also require a 1-year or 3-year commitment to achieve their discount, making them uneconomical and inflexible for a workload running only a few months.

260.text:
Option B is correct because Reserved Instances provide a significant discount, up to about 72 percent compared to On-Demand, in exchange for a 1-year or 3-year commitment to a consistent amount of compute. For a stateful workload that must run continuously for 3 years, a 3-year Reserved Instance commitment delivers the lowest cost while guaranteeing capacity, making it the most cost-effective choice.

Option A is incorrect because On-Demand Instances charge full pay-as-you-go rates with no commitment discount, which is far more expensive for a steady, long-running workload. Option C is incorrect because Dedicated Instances run on isolated hardware at a price premium, addressing compliance needs rather than minimizing cost. Option D is incorrect because Spot Instances can be interrupted with little notice when AWS reclaims capacity, making them unsuitable for a stateful workload that must run reliably for three years despite their low price.

261.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Macie is a data security service that uses machine learning and pattern matching to discover, classify, and protect sensitive data stored in Amazon S3, such as personally identifiable information and financial data. It automatically identifies which data should be considered sensitive and reports findings, precisely meeting the company's requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses workloads for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure, not the sensitivity classification of stored data. Option C is incorrect because AWS IAM manages identities and access permissions; it controls who can access resources but does not analyze data content for sensitivity. Option D is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch monitors metrics, logs, and operational performance, and does not inspect or classify stored data to determine whether it is sensitive.

262.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Shield is a managed Distributed Denial of Service protection service. Shield Standard automatically protects all AWS customers against common network and transport layer DDoS attacks at no extra cost, and Shield Advanced adds enhanced protection, detection, and response for larger and more sophisticated attacks, directly defending the company's website against DDoS attacks.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Resource Access Manager shares resources securely across AWS accounts and has no role in attack mitigation. Option B is incorrect because AWS Amplify is a framework for building and deploying full-stack web and mobile applications, not a security or DDoS defense service. Option D is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes logs to identify malicious activity and generate findings, but it detects rather than actively mitigates DDoS attacks, which is Shield's purpose.

263.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Config continuously tracks and records the configuration state of AWS resources over time, maintaining a configuration history and enabling auditing of how resources change. It can evaluate configurations against desired rules and show what changed, when, and how, which exactly matches the need to track, record, and audit configuration changes.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Shield provides DDoS protection and does not track resource configuration changes. Option C is incorrect because AWS IAM manages users, roles, and permissions for controlling access, not for recording configuration history of resources. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs automated security and vulnerability assessments of workloads; it identifies vulnerabilities rather than auditing and recording configuration changes to AWS resources.

264.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Cognito enables web and mobile applications to add user sign-up, sign-in, and access control, and it supports identity federation with social identity providers such as Facebook, Google, Apple, and Amazon. This allows the company's online shopping website to let customers log in using their existing social media credentials without building a custom identity store.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Directory Service provides managed Microsoft Active Directory for enterprise directory needs, not federation with consumer social media logins. Option B is incorrect because AWS IAM manages access for AWS accounts and resources by administrators and applications, not end-user sign-in through social providers. Option D is incorrect because AWS IAM Identity Center provides single sign-on for workforce users to access AWS accounts and business applications, whereas Cognito is the service designed for customer-facing application authentication with social IdPs.

265.text:
Options A and B are correct because Amazon S3 protects objects at rest using server-side encryption. SSE-S3 (option A) encrypts each object with a unique AES-256 key that is itself encrypted by a regularly rotated master key, fully managed by S3 with no key administration required. SSE-KMS (option B) integrates with AWS Key Management Service so you control the customer master key, gain an audit trail through CloudTrail, and can apply key policies and rotation, giving stronger governance for compliance needs.

Options C and D are incorrect because TLS and SSL are protocols that encrypt data in transit between clients and S3, not data at rest stored on disk; they protect the network connection, not the persisted object. Option E is incorrect because Transparent Data Encryption is a database-level encryption feature associated with engines such as Microsoft SQL Server and Oracle, not an Amazon S3 at-rest encryption option.

266.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Artifact is the central, self-service portal for on-demand access to AWS security and compliance reports. It provides downloadable documents such as SOC reports, PCI DSS attestations, and ISO certifications, which you can submit directly to auditors or regulators as evidence of AWS's compliance posture. It also hosts agreements like the AWS Business Associate Addendum.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Certificate Manager provisions and manages SSL/TLS certificates for encrypting traffic, not compliance documents. Option B is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager provides operational management and automation for resources, such as patching and run commands, not audit artifacts. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs automated vulnerability and security assessments of workloads; it generates its own findings but does not supply AWS's formal compliance reports for auditors.

267.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to securely store, encrypt, and manage secrets such as database credentials, API keys, and tokens. It encrypts secrets at rest using AWS KMS, controls access through IAM policies, and uniquely supports automatic rotation of database passwords by integrating directly with services like Amazon RDS, reducing the risk of long-lived static credentials.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Shield is a managed DDoS protection service and does not store credentials. Option C is incorrect because IAM manages users, roles, and permissions for AWS access but is not designed to store application or database passwords. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Cognito provides authentication and user identity management for web and mobile applications, not secure storage of backend database passwords.

268.text:
Option B is correct because security groups act as a virtual, instance-level (technically ENI-level) firewall that controls inbound and outbound traffic for Amazon EC2 instances. They are stateful, meaning that if inbound traffic is allowed, the corresponding return traffic is automatically permitted, and they support only allow rules, evaluated as a whole before a decision is made.

Option A is incorrect because a network access control list operates at the subnet level rather than the instance level, and it is stateless, requiring explicit rules for both inbound and return traffic. Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor provides best-practice recommendations across cost, performance, and security, not traffic filtering. Option D is incorrect because virtual private gateways are the VPC-side endpoint for VPN connections to on-premises networks, not firewalls.

269.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Personalize is a fully managed machine learning service that creates individualized product and content recommendations based on a company's customer data. It uses the same technology developed at Amazon.com to build, train, and deploy recommendation models without requiring machine learning expertise, delivering real-time personalization through a simple API.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Polly converts text into lifelike speech and has no recommendation capability. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Comprehend is a natural language processing service that extracts insights such as sentiment and entities from text, not product recommendations. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Rekognition analyzes images and videos for objects, faces, and scenes, which is unrelated to generating customer recommendations.

270.text:
Options A and C are correct because the People perspective of the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework focuses on the human and organizational change needed for cloud adoption. Organizational alignment (option A) addresses structuring teams and leadership support around cloud goals, while organization design (option C) addresses defining roles, structures, and operating models that enable cloud transformation and continuous workforce development.

Option B is incorrect because portfolio management belongs to the Business perspective, which aligns IT investments with business outcomes. Option D is incorrect because risk management is part of the Governance perspective, which oversees control, compliance, and cloud financial management. Option E is incorrect because modern application development is associated with the Platform perspective, which builds and maintains the enterprise cloud platform and engineering practices, not the People perspective.

271.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Storage Gateway is a hybrid cloud storage service that connects on-premises environments to virtually unlimited AWS cloud storage. It presents familiar protocols such as NFS, SMB, and iSCSI to local users and applications while seamlessly backing the data to services like Amazon S3 and Amazon S3 Glacier, with local caching for low-latency access to frequently used data.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DataSync is a data transfer service for moving large datasets between on-premises and AWS, not a continuously presented storage layer. Option B is incorrect because Amazon S3 Glacier is a low-cost cloud archive storage class, not a hybrid gateway. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides block storage volumes attached to EC2 instances within AWS, not on-premises access to cloud storage.

272.text:
Options A and D are correct because together they cover both account-specific and broad service health. The AWS Personal Health Dashboard (option A), now part of AWS Health, provides personalized alerts about events that affect the company's specific resources and accounts. The AWS Service Health Dashboard (option D) shows the general, public status of AWS services across all Regions, confirming whether the underlying infrastructure is operating normally.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager manages and automates operational tasks on your resources, not AWS service availability. Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor offers best-practice recommendations on cost, security, and performance, not real-time infrastructure status. Option E is incorrect because AWS Service Catalog lets organizations create and manage approved catalogs of products for deployment, which is unrelated to monitoring infrastructure health.

273.text:
Option D is correct because Migration Evaluator (formerly TSO Logic) is purpose-built to provide a customized, data-driven assessment of an organization's existing on-premises environment. It analyzes server and utilization inventory to produce a business case that projects expected running costs in the AWS Cloud, helping the company plan and justify migration with right-sized recommendations.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor evaluates existing AWS resources for cost and best-practice optimization, not on-premises environments. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs automated security vulnerability assessments of AWS workloads, not cost projections. Option C is incorrect because AWS Control Tower sets up and governs a secure multi-account AWS environment, providing landing zones and guardrails rather than on-premises cost assessments.

274.text:
Option B is correct because an Availability Zone is one or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity within an AWS Region. Each Availability Zone is physically separated and isolated to provide fault tolerance, and architecting across multiple Availability Zones is the foundation for building highly available, resilient applications.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network that caches content at globally distributed edge locations, not a data center environment. Option C is incorrect because a VPC is a logically isolated virtual network within AWS, a software construct rather than a physical environment of data centers. Option D is incorrect because AWS Outposts extends AWS infrastructure to a customer's own on-premises facility; it is not the AWS construct defined as one or more data centers.

275.text:
Option B is correct because the Amazon EC2 instance store provides temporary, ephemeral block-level storage that is physically attached to the host computer. Because the data resides on disks local to the host, it is lost whenever the instance is stopped, hibernated, or terminated, making it suitable only for temporary data such as caches, buffers, or scratch data.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides persistent block storage that retains data independently of the instance lifecycle and survives stops and terminations when not set to delete on termination. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EFS is a durable, persistent shared file system. Option D is incorrect because Amazon S3 is durable object storage designed for 99.999999999 percent durability and is entirely independent of any EC2 instance state.

276.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon FSx File Gateway provides on-premises workloads with low-latency, native access to fully managed Windows file shares stored in Amazon FSx for Windows File Server. It supports the SMB protocol and local caching, and as a managed gateway it minimizes the need to provision and maintain additional file server infrastructure in the data center.

Option B is incorrect because AWS DataSync transfers and synchronizes data between locations but does not continuously present Windows file shares for ongoing access. Option C is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage and does not natively provide SMB Windows file shares. Option D is incorrect because the AWS Snow Family is used for offline, physical bulk data transfer and edge computing, not for live access to Windows file shares from on premises.

277.text:
Option C is correct because pay-as-you-go pricing is the AWS benefit that lets companies pay only for the individual services they consume, for as long as they use them, without long-term contracts. This directly replaces large upfront fixed capital expenditures with variable operational expenses that scale with actual usage, improving agility and avoiding over-provisioning.

Option A is incorrect because high availability describes the resilience and uptime of systems through redundancy, not a pricing benefit. Option B is incorrect because economies of scale refers to AWS aggregating usage across many customers to lower per-unit costs, which is related but does not itself describe replacing fixed expenses with variable ones. Option D is incorrect because global reach refers to deploying applications across worldwide Regions, not a cost or expense model.

278.text:
Option A is correct because the Amazon S3 Inventory report provides a scheduled, flat-file output that lists objects and their associated metadata, including object tags, bucket name, prefixes, encryption status, and storage class. This is ideal for tracking and auditing objects across buckets at scale without making individual List API calls for each object.

Option B is incorrect because S3 Lifecycle automates transitioning objects between storage classes or expiring them, not reporting on tags and prefixes. Option C is incorrect because S3 Versioning preserves multiple variants of objects to protect against accidental overwrite or deletion, not inventory tracking. Option D is incorrect because S3 ACLs are a legacy access-control mechanism that grants permissions to buckets and objects, not a feature for listing or reporting on object metadata.

279.text:
Option C is correct because AWS IAM Identity Center (successor to AWS Single Sign-On) lets users authenticate once with a single set of credentials and then access and be authorized across multiple AWS accounts and applications. It centrally manages access to accounts in AWS Organizations and can connect to existing identity providers, providing federated single sign-on with permission sets.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Organizations centrally manages and governs multiple accounts with consolidated billing and policies but does not itself provide user single sign-on. Option B is incorrect because an IAM user provides credentials scoped to a single account, not centralized access across many accounts. Option D is incorrect because AWS Control Tower sets up and governs a multi-account landing zone; it relies on IAM Identity Center for sign-on rather than providing it directly.

280.text:
Option B is correct because security groups control inbound and outbound network traffic at the instance level for Amazon EC2 instances. They function as stateful virtual firewalls attached to the instance's network interface, automatically allowing return traffic for permitted connections and using allow rules to specify which ports, protocols, and sources are permitted.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Shield provides managed protection against DDoS attacks, not granular instance-level traffic control. Option C is incorrect because Network Access Analyzer is a diagnostic tool that identifies unintended network access paths in a VPC; it analyzes connectivity but does not enforce instance traffic rules. Option D is incorrect because VPC endpoints enable private connectivity to AWS services without traversing the internet, rather than acting as an instance-level firewall.

281.text:
Option A is correct because a Multi-Region architecture deploys an application across two or more AWS Regions worldwide, which is the appropriate model for a global deployment. This approach places resources closer to users in different geographies to reduce latency, meets data residency requirements, and provides resilience against the failure of an entire Region.

Option B is incorrect because a Single-Region deployment confines the application to one geographic Region and cannot serve a truly global user base optimally. Options C and D are incorrect because Multi-AZ and Single-AZ describe deployment within a single Region across one or more Availability Zones; they improve in-Region availability but do not provide the global, cross-Region distribution required for a worldwide deployment.

282.text:
Option A is correct because Spot Instances let you use spare Amazon EC2 capacity at discounts of up to 90 percent compared to On-Demand prices, the largest available discount. Because AWS can reclaim this capacity with a short notification when it is needed elsewhere, Spot Instances are ideal specifically for fault-tolerant, flexible workloads that can tolerate interruptions.

Option B is incorrect because Convertible Reserved Instances offer discounts of roughly up to 54 percent in exchange for a one or three year commitment, which is less than Spot. Option C is incorrect because Standard Reserved Instances offer up to about 72 percent off but still require a term commitment and are smaller than Spot's discount. Option D is incorrect because Dedicated Hosts are physical servers dedicated to your use, which are among the most expensive options, not the cheapest.

283.text:
Option A is correct because an internet gateway is the VPC component that allows communication between resources in a VPC and the internet, enabling inbound traffic from the internet to reach resources with public IP addresses. It is a horizontally scaled, redundant, and highly available component that performs network address translation for instances assigned public IPv4 addresses.

Option B is incorrect because a NAT gateway allows instances in private subnets to initiate outbound connections to the internet while preventing unsolicited inbound connections. Option C is incorrect because AWS WAF is a web application firewall that filters malicious HTTP/HTTPS requests but does not itself provide internet connectivity. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering connects two VPCs privately for internal traffic and does not route traffic to or from the public internet.

284.text:
Option C is correct because designing for automated recovery from failure is a core well-architected design principle, central to the Reliability pillar. Rather than relying on manual intervention, you monitor systems and automatically trigger recovery actions, such as replacing failed instances or routing around impaired components, which improves resilience and reduces downtime and human error.

Option A is incorrect because the principle is to bring compute closer to data or use edge caching where appropriate, but the broad statement here is not a recognized design principle. Option B is incorrect because provisioning for peak capacity wastes resources; the cloud principle is to scale elastically to match demand. Option D is incorrect because well-architected designs favor loosely coupled components to improve scalability and fault isolation, not tightly coupled ones.

285.text:
Options A and D are correct because both describe genuine cost advantages of the AWS Cloud. AWS replaces large upfront capital expenditures with variable pay-as-you-go costs (option A), so companies pay only for what they use and avoid over-provisioning. AWS also leverages economies of scale (option D); as its aggregate usage grows, it achieves higher efficiencies and passes savings to customers through continually reduced prices, lowering total cost of ownership.

Option B is incorrect because, while AWS is designed for high availability, it cannot guarantee the complete elimination of all user downtime. Option C is incorrect because moving to AWS reduces the burden of managing physical infrastructure but does not eliminate the need for IT staff to manage cloud operations. Option E is incorrect because AWS offers multiple EC2 pricing models, such as On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, and Savings Plans, not a single pricing model.

286.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Outposts extends native AWS infrastructure, services, and APIs to a customer's own on-premises data center using AWS-managed hardware. This keeps data local on premises to satisfy regulatory and data residency requirements while providing very low latency between the on-premises workloads and the AWS environment running on the Outpost.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Local Zones place AWS infrastructure closer to large population centers but are still AWS-operated facilities, not the customer's premises. Option B is incorrect because Availability Zones are data centers within an AWS Region, not on-premises. Option D is incorrect because AWS Wavelength Zones embed AWS compute within telecommunications providers' 5G networks for mobile edge applications, not within the company's own data center for data residency.

287.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EC2 Dedicated Hosts provide a physical server fully dedicated to your use, giving you visibility into and control over the underlying sockets and physical cores. This is the option required to use existing per-socket, per-core, or per-VM software licenses, such as Microsoft Windows Server, under Bring Your Own License terms while remaining compliant.

Option A is incorrect because Spot Instances use spare capacity for cost savings and do not expose the host's physical characteristics for licensing. Option B is incorrect because Dedicated Instances run on hardware dedicated to a single account but do not provide the host-level socket and core visibility needed for these license types. Option D is incorrect because Reserved Instances are a billing discount commitment, not a way to address physical-server software licensing.

288.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Storage Gateway provides on-premises applications with low-latency access to data stored in the AWS Cloud. It maintains a local cache of frequently accessed data on premises while durably storing the full dataset in services such as Amazon S3, so applications retrieve hot data quickly over standard protocols while benefiting from virtually unlimited cloud storage.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network that caches content at edge locations to accelerate delivery to end users, not on-premises application access to cloud data. Option C is incorrect because AWS Backup centrally manages and automates backups across AWS services, not low-latency local access. Option D is incorrect because AWS DataSync moves data in bulk between on-premises and AWS but does not provide ongoing low-latency cached access for applications.

289.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network (CDN) that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to users globally with low latency and high transfer speeds. It caches content at a worldwide network of edge locations, so requests are served from the location nearest the user, and it integrates with AWS Shield, AWS WAF, and TLS for secure delivery.

Option A is incorrect because automatically scaling resources from a unified interface describes services such as AWS Auto Scaling, not a CDN. Option C is incorrect because directly managing global traffic with routing types like latency-based routing, geo DNS, geoproximity, and weighted round robin describes Amazon Route 53, the DNS service. Option D is incorrect because automatically distributing incoming traffic across multiple targets such as EC2 instances, containers, IP addresses, and Lambda functions describes Elastic Load Balancing, not CloudFront.

290.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration uses Amazon CloudFront edge locations and the optimized AWS backbone network to accelerate uploads to and downloads from S3 over long distances. End users connect to a nearby edge location, and traffic is routed to the destination bucket over Amazon's private network, reducing latency for geographically distant transfers.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Cross-Region Replication asynchronously copies objects between buckets in different Regions for compliance and resilience; it does not reduce end-user-to-S3 latency via edge locations. Option C is incorrect because S3 Event Notifications trigger workflows (to SNS, SQS, or Lambda) when objects change and have nothing to do with transfer speed. Option D is incorrect because S3 Standard-Infrequent Access is a lower-cost storage class for less-frequently accessed data; it changes pricing and retrieval characteristics, not network latency through edge locations.

291.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon DynamoDB is AWS's fully managed, serverless NoSQL database service. It provides key-value and document data models with single-digit-millisecond performance at any scale, automatic scaling, and built-in high availability and durability across multiple Availability Zones, with no servers to provision or manage.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Aurora is a relational (SQL) database engine compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL, not a NoSQL store. Option C is incorrect because Amazon RDS is a managed service for relational databases such as MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and SQL Server, which are also SQL-based. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a relational, columnar data warehouse designed for analytics and online analytical processing (OLAP) queries, not a NoSQL operational database.

292.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Aurora is a fully managed relational database engine that is wire-compatible with both MySQL and PostgreSQL. It delivers up to several times the throughput of standard MySQL and PostgreSQL while providing automated storage scaling, six-way replication across three Availability Zones, and automated backups, allowing existing MySQL or PostgreSQL applications to migrate with minimal changes.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a columnar data warehouse for analytics, not a MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible transactional database. Option B is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value and document database, not a relational engine. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Neptune is a managed graph database optimized for highly connected datasets and graph query languages such as Gremlin and SPARQL, not relational SQL workloads compatible with MySQL or PostgreSQL.

293.text:
Option D is correct because loosely coupling components is the architecture design principle that isolates failures between dependent parts of a system. By inserting intermediaries such as queues (Amazon SQS), notification topics (Amazon SNS), or load balancers between components, a failure in one component does not directly cascade to others, improving fault tolerance and allowing components to scale and fail independently.

Option A is incorrect because a monolithic design tightly couples components, so a single failure can bring down the entire application. Option B is incorrect because designing for automation improves consistency and recovery speed but does not by itself isolate failures between dependent components. Option C is incorrect because designing for single points of failure is the opposite of a sound principle; the goal is to eliminate single points of failure, not embrace them.

294.text:
Option B is correct because global reach is the cloud computing benefit that lets a company deploy applications close to users worldwide through AWS's network of Regions, Availability Zones, and edge locations. This global infrastructure reduces latency and improves the user experience by serving traffic from locations geographically near end users, often with just a few clicks and no need to build physical data centers.

Option A is incorrect because economy of scale refers to lower costs achieved as AWS aggregates usage across many customers, not to worldwide deployment. Option C is incorrect because agility describes the ability to quickly provision and experiment with resources, not the geographic distribution of applications. Option D is incorrect because high availability refers to designing systems to remain operational despite failures; while global infrastructure supports it, the specific benefit of reaching users worldwide is global reach.

295.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon CloudWatch is AWS's monitoring and observability service that makes it easier to monitor and troubleshoot application logs and cloud resources. It collects metrics, ingests and queries logs through CloudWatch Logs, sets alarms on thresholds, and visualizes data on dashboards, giving operators a unified view to detect and diagnose issues across AWS resources and applications.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EC2 provides virtual server compute capacity; it is a resource to be monitored, not the monitoring service. Option B is incorrect because AWS IAM manages users, roles, and permissions for access control, not log and resource monitoring. Option D is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records API activity and account events for auditing and governance; it tracks who did what, whereas CloudWatch focuses on operational metrics, logs, and troubleshooting application and resource health.

296.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes Amazon EC2 instance utilization metrics, such as CPU, memory, network, and disk, and uses machine learning to recommend optimal instance types and sizes. This helps reduce cost and improve performance by identifying over-provisioned and under-provisioned EC2 instances (and also EC2 Auto Scaling groups, EBS volumes, and Lambda functions).

Option B is incorrect because Amazon RDS is not the service whose sizing is driven by Compute Optimizer in this context; RDS right-sizing is handled through its own tooling and metrics. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Lightsail uses fixed, bundled instance plans rather than Compute Optimizer workload-based recommendations. Option D is incorrect because AWS Step Functions is a serverless workflow orchestration service with no instances to size, so Compute Optimizer recommendations do not apply to it.

297.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Application Discovery Service helps plan migrations by collecting configuration, usage, and behavior data from on-premises data centers, including server specifications, performance metrics, running processes, and network dependencies. This inventory and dependency mapping lets a company group servers into applications and accurately scope and prioritize their migration to AWS.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Resource Groups organizes and manages existing AWS resources by tags; it does not gather on-premises data center information. Option C is incorrect because AWS Service Catalog lets organizations create and manage catalogs of approved IT products for deployment, not discover on-premises environments. Option D is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager provides operational management and automation for resources, primarily within AWS and managed instances, and is not the dedicated tool for collecting on-premises discovery data for migration planning.

298.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) is a fully managed pub/sub messaging service built on publishers and subscribers. Publishers send messages to an SNS topic, and the topic fans those messages out to all subscribed endpoints, such as Amazon SQS queues, AWS Lambda functions, HTTP/S endpoints, email, and SMS, decoupling message producers from consumers.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Lambda runs event-driven code in response to triggers and is a compute service, not a publish/subscribe messaging service. Option C is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch monitors metrics, logs, and alarms; although it can send notifications, it is not itself a publisher/subscriber messaging system. Option D is incorrect because AWS CloudFormation provisions and manages infrastructure as code through templates and stacks, which has nothing to do with the publisher/subscriber messaging pattern.

299.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Pricing Calculator lets a company model and estimate the monthly and total cost of ownership for AWS services before deployment. The company can configure planned Amazon EC2 instances and associated storage to generate a detailed, forward-looking cost estimate, making it ideal for budgeting during the early planning stages of a migration.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Compute Optimizer recommends right-sized resources based on metrics from workloads already running in AWS, so it cannot estimate costs for future, not-yet-deployed instances. Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor analyzes an existing AWS environment for cost, security, and performance optimizations, not predicted costs for planned resources. Option D is incorrect because AWS Application Migration Service automates the lift-and-shift migration of servers to AWS; it performs migration, not cost forecasting.

300.text:
Option C is correct because AWS CloudTrail records account activity and API calls made against AWS resources, capturing details such as the identity of the caller, the time of the call, the source IP address, and the request parameters. This provides the governance, compliance, and operational and risk auditing the company needs to know when and by whom an API call was made.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch focuses on performance metrics, logs, and alarms for monitoring resource health rather than recording the API calls themselves. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs automated security vulnerability assessments of workloads, not API activity logging. Option D is incorrect because AWS IAM manages identities and permissions that control who can make API calls, but it does not record a history of the calls that were actually made.

301.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) is a fully managed message queuing service that lets application components send, store, and receive messages reliably between distributed components. SQS offers FIFO (first-in, first-out) queues that guarantee messages are processed in the exact order they are sent and provide exactly-once processing, directly satisfying both the messaging and ordering requirements.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Step Functions orchestrates workflows by coordinating service calls into state machines; it is not a message queue for storing and receiving messages. Option B is incorrect because Amazon SNS is a publish/subscribe service that pushes messages to subscribers and does not store messages in a queue for components to retrieve. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Kinesis Data Streams handles real-time streaming data ordered within shards, but the service purpose-built for FIFO message queuing between components is SQS.

302.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Artifact is the central, self-service portal for on-demand access to AWS security and compliance reports and select online agreements. Customers can download artifacts such as SOC reports, PCI DSS reports, and ISO certifications at any time to share with auditors and regulators, satisfying the requirement to retrieve compliance reports on demand.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Secrets Manager securely stores and rotates secrets such as database credentials and API keys, not compliance documentation. Option C is incorrect because AWS Security Hub aggregates and prioritizes security findings across accounts; it assesses security posture rather than providing downloadable AWS compliance reports. Option D is incorrect because AWS Certificate Manager provisions and manages SSL/TLS certificates, which is unrelated to retrieving compliance reports.

303.text:
Option A is correct because VPC Flow Logs capture information about the inbound and outbound IP traffic going to and from network interfaces within an Amazon VPC. The logs record details such as source and destination addresses, ports, protocols, packet and byte counts, and accept/reject decisions, which can be published to Amazon CloudWatch Logs or Amazon S3 for monitoring, troubleshooting, and security analysis.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector scans workloads for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure; it does not capture VPC traffic flow records. Option C is incorrect because VPC endpoint services (PrivateLink) provide private connectivity to services without traversing the internet, not traffic logging. Option D is incorrect because a NAT gateway enables instances in private subnets to reach the internet for outbound traffic; it routes traffic but does not capture flow log information.

304.text:
Option B is correct because under the AWS shared responsibility model, the customer is responsible for security in the cloud, which includes protecting their application data. This covers data classification, encryption (in transit and at rest), integrity, and access controls such as IAM policies, all of which remain the customer's responsibility regardless of the AWS services used.

Option A is incorrect because maintenance of the underlying hardware for EC2 instances is AWS's responsibility as part of securing the infrastructure (security of the cloud). Option C is incorrect because the physical security of data centers is managed entirely by AWS. Option D is incorrect because the maintenance of the underlying VPC infrastructure components is AWS's responsibility; the customer configures their VPC settings, but the foundational network hardware and software are operated by AWS.

305.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Athena is a serverless, interactive query service that lets a developer use standard SQL to query data directly in Amazon S3, including across many CSV files at once. There is no infrastructure to provision or manage and pricing is based on data scanned, so generating a summary report from all the files involves minimal operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 Select queries data within a single object, not across many files, making it unsuitable for summarizing all the reports together. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Redshift requires provisioning and loading data into a cluster (or managing Serverless capacity), adding more setup and overhead than simply querying the files in place. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EC2 would require provisioning, configuring, and maintaining servers and query software, which is the highest operational overhead of the options.

306.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CodeStar provides a unified interface to quickly set up an entire continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) toolchain. It uses project templates that automatically provision and integrate AWS developer services such as CodeCommit, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, and CodePipeline, letting a company stand up a working pipeline in minutes with minimal configuration.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Config records and evaluates the configuration of AWS resources for compliance, not for building CI/CD pipelines. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Cognito provides user authentication, authorization, and identity management for applications, which is unrelated to release automation. Option C is incorrect because AWS DataSync automates and accelerates moving data between on-premises storage and AWS storage services, not software delivery pipelines.

307.text:
Option C is correct because the operational excellence pillar of the AWS Well-Architected Framework focuses on running and monitoring systems and continuously improving processes and procedures. A key design principle of this pillar is to make frequent, small, reversible changes, which reduces the blast radius of any single change and allows components to be updated regularly and safely rolled back if needed.

Option A is incorrect because the security pillar centers on protecting data, systems, and assets through controls such as identity management and encryption. Option B is incorrect because the performance efficiency pillar focuses on using computing resources efficiently and adapting as demand and technologies change. Option D is incorrect because the reliability pillar emphasizes a workload's ability to recover from failures and meet demand, rather than the practice of making small, reversible, regular changes.

308.text:
Option D is correct because global reach is the cloud computing advantage a company applies when it uses multiple AWS Regions to deploy applications closer to users in different countries, increasing availability and reducing latency. AWS's worldwide infrastructure lets the company serve a global user base without building physical data centers in each location.

Option A is incorrect because pay-as-you-go pricing refers to paying only for the resources consumed, not to geographic distribution of applications. Option B is incorrect because capacity forecasting (or eliminating the need to guess capacity) relates to provisioning resources to match demand, not to serving users across countries. Option C is incorrect because economies of scale describes the lower per-unit costs AWS achieves and passes on through aggregated usage, which is a cost advantage rather than the ability to reach users worldwide.

309.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Snowball Edge is a physical edge computing and data transfer device designed to collect and process data locally and then ship the data to AWS. It is ideal for environments with limited, unreliable, or intermittent network connectivity, allowing a company to migrate 10 TB without depending on a continuous network connection, and it can run local compute to process the data before transfer.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DMS migrates databases over a network connection and depends on reliable connectivity. Option B is incorrect because AWS DataSync transfers data over the network between storage systems and also requires stable connectivity, making it unsuitable for intermittent links. Option C is incorrect because AWS Backup centralizes and automates backups of AWS resources; it is not a service for collecting and physically transferring on-premises data under poor connectivity.

310.text:
Option D is correct because AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) is a managed service that protects web applications and APIs from common exploits such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting by inspecting incoming HTTP/S requests against configurable and managed rules. It also integrates with Amazon CloudWatch and supports detailed web ACL traffic logging, providing the access logging the company needs for its ecommerce applications.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Network Firewall provides network and transport layer filtering for VPC traffic rather than application-layer protection against web exploits like SQL injection. Option B is incorrect because Amazon RDS for SQL Server is a managed relational database, not a security service for blocking injection attacks. Option C is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes activity for malicious behavior; it detects and alerts but does not actively block SQL injection at the application layer.

311.text:
Options C and E are correct because both VPC peering and AWS Transit Gateway create network connections between VPCs. VPC peering establishes a direct, one-to-one private connection between two VPCs so resources can communicate using private IP addresses. AWS Transit Gateway acts as a central cloud router (hub-and-spoke) that connects many VPCs and on-premises networks through a single gateway, simplifying connectivity at scale and avoiding the complex mesh of point-to-point peering connections.

Option A is incorrect because VPC endpoints provide private connectivity from a VPC to AWS services or PrivateLink-powered services, not VPC-to-VPC connections. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Route 53 is a DNS and traffic-routing service and does not establish network connectivity between VPCs. Option D is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect creates a dedicated private network link between an on-premises data center and AWS, not a connection between two VPCs.

312.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Marketplace is a curated digital catalog where customers can find, buy, deploy, and manage third-party software, data products, and services that run on AWS. It streamlines procurement with flexible pricing and consolidated AWS billing, making it the appropriate service for purchasing third-party software for a workload on AWS.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Resource Access Manager shares AWS resources across accounts and does not sell software. Option B is incorrect because AWS Managed Services provides ongoing operational management of AWS infrastructure on a customer's behalf, not a marketplace to purchase software. Option C is incorrect because AWS License Manager helps track and manage software licenses to maintain compliance, but the actual purchase of third-party software is done through AWS Marketplace.

313.text:
Option B is correct because security groups act as virtual stateful firewalls that attach directly to specific Amazon EC2 instances (specifically to their elastic network interfaces). They control inbound and outbound traffic by allowing rules based on protocol, port, and source/destination, and because they are stateful, return traffic is automatically permitted. This makes them the proper mechanism for applying security rules to specific EC2 instances.

Option A is incorrect because network ACLs are stateless firewalls that apply at the subnet level, affecting all instances in the subnet rather than specific instances. Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor provides best-practice recommendations across cost, performance, security, and fault tolerance, but it does not enforce traffic rules on instances. Option D is incorrect because AWS WAF protects web applications at the HTTP/HTTPS layer (typically fronted by CloudFront, ALB, or API Gateway) against web exploits, not instance-level network access.

314.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated, private physical network connection between a company's on-premises corporate network and AWS. It bypasses the public internet, providing consistent low latency, higher bandwidth, and more predictable network performance, which is exactly what a private network connection between AWS and a corporate data center requires.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Connect is a cloud-based contact center service for customer interactions, not a networking connectivity service. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Route 53 is a DNS and domain registration service; it resolves names to addresses but does not create a private link to AWS. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering connects two Amazon VPCs to each other within AWS, not an on-premises corporate network to AWS. A Site-to-Site VPN or Direct Connect is required to reach on-premises networks, and Direct Connect best fits the private, dedicated requirement.

315.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Snowmobile is an exabyte-scale data transfer service delivered as a 45-foot ruggedized shipping container that can move up to 100 petabytes per unit. For a one-time migration of 75 petabytes, Snowmobile is the most cost-effective option, as transferring that volume over a network connection would take many years and incur substantial bandwidth costs.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Snowball Edge Storage Optimized devices hold roughly 80 TB of usable capacity each, so moving 75 PB would require hundreds of devices, making it operationally impractical and less cost-effective at this scale. Option C is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect provides ongoing network connectivity, but transferring 75 PB over even a high-bandwidth link would take an impractical amount of time and recurring cost. Option D is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway is designed for hybrid storage integration and incremental data access, not bulk one-time petabyte-scale migrations.

316.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon QuickSight Q is a natural language processing feature within Amazon QuickSight that lets users type plain-language questions and instantly receive answers with relevant, automatically generated visualizations in their BI dashboards. This directly meets the requirement to ask questions and get answers with visualizations.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Macie uses machine learning to discover and protect sensitive data such as personally identifiable information in Amazon S3, not to power BI dashboards. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Rekognition analyzes images and video for objects, faces, and text; it is unrelated to natural language BI queries. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Lex builds conversational interfaces such as chatbots using voice and text, but it does not integrate natural language querying into BI dashboards or generate data visualizations like QuickSight Q does.

317.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon EC2 Spot Instances let a company use spare, unused EC2 capacity at discounts of up to 90 percent compared to On-Demand pricing. Because Spot Instances can be interrupted with short notice when AWS reclaims capacity, they are ideal for fault-tolerant, stateless workloads that can handle interruptions, which matches the described scenario and optimizes cost.

Option B is incorrect because Dedicated Instances run on hardware dedicated to a single customer and carry a price premium for isolation, not cost savings on spare capacity. Option C is incorrect because Reserved Instances reduce cost through a one- or three-year commitment to steady-state usage, which does not fit a company seeking to consume occasional unused capacity. Option D is incorrect because On-Demand Instances are the standard full-price, pay-as-you-go option with no discount, offering no cost optimization for the stateless, interruptible workloads described.

318.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a platform-as-a-service that handles the deployment, management, scaling, and monitoring of applications in the AWS Cloud. Developers simply upload their code, and Elastic Beanstalk automatically provisions and manages the underlying capacity, load balancing, and health monitoring, directly supporting application deployment and management.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CodeGuru uses machine learning to provide automated code reviews and application performance recommendations, not application deployment. Option B is incorrect because AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for running containers; it executes container workloads but is not itself a full application deployment and management platform. Option C is incorrect because AWS CodeCommit is a managed source-control service for hosting Git repositories, which stores code but does not deploy or manage running applications. Elastic Beanstalk is the service purpose-built to deploy and manage applications.

319.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon S3 is an object storage service designed for 99.999999999 percent (eleven nines) of durability. It automatically and redundantly stores objects across multiple devices and Availability Zones within a Region, making it the AWS service known for highly durable object storage.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon EFS is a managed file storage service using the NFS protocol for shared file access from Linux instances; it is file storage, not object storage. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides block-level storage volumes attached to individual EC2 instances; it is block storage, not object storage, and its durability model differs. Option D is incorrect because Amazon FSx provides fully managed third-party file systems such as Windows File Server and Lustre, which are file storage offerings rather than highly durable object storage like Amazon S3.

320.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EC2 provides flexible virtual server capacity that can run a data-processing job for its full duration without time limits. A weekly job that takes about five hours fits naturally on EC2, and the company can launch the instance for the job and stop it afterward to control cost.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Lambda has a maximum function timeout of 15 minutes, so it cannot run a single workload that takes roughly five hours. Option C is incorrect because AWS CodeDeploy automates the deployment of application code to compute services; it does not perform data-processing computation. Option D is incorrect because AWS Wavelength embeds AWS compute and storage within telecommunications providers' 5G networks to deliver ultra-low-latency applications to mobile devices, which is unrelated to a periodic batch data-processing task. EC2 is the appropriate general-purpose compute choice here.

321.text:
Option A is correct because security groups are virtual stateful firewalls attached to Amazon EC2 instances that control traffic by allowing rules based on protocol, port range, and source. Configuring a security group to permit traffic only on a specific port is a core, supported task that restricts instance access to a defined port.

Option B is incorrect because denying access to malicious IP addresses at the subnet level is the function of network ACLs, which operate at the subnet boundary and support explicit deny rules; security groups support only allow rules and apply per instance. Option C is incorrect because protecting cached CloudFront data is unrelated to security groups. Option D is incorrect because security groups are stateful, automatically allowing return traffic, whereas stateless firewalling that evaluates each direction independently is a property of network ACLs, not security groups.

322.text:
Option B is correct because the Operational Excellence pillar of the AWS Well-Architected Framework focuses on running and monitoring systems to deliver business value and on continually improving supporting processes and procedures. Its goals include reducing management overhead through automation, performing operations as code, and refining processes, which precisely match the company's desire to run workloads effectively, reduce overhead, and improve processes.

Option A is incorrect because the Reliability pillar focuses on a workload's ability to recover from failures and meet demand, not process improvement. Option C is incorrect because the Performance Efficiency pillar focuses on using computing resources efficiently and adapting as demand changes, not on reducing operational overhead and improving processes. Option D is incorrect because the Cost Optimization pillar focuses on avoiding unnecessary costs and delivering maximum business value at the lowest price point, which is distinct from the operational improvement goals described.

323.text:
Option B is correct because AWS WAF is a web application firewall that monitors and filters HTTP and HTTPS requests to web applications. It uses configurable rules, including AWS Managed Rules, to block common web exploits such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS) before they reach the application, directly meeting the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs automated vulnerability assessments of EC2 instances and container images; it identifies software vulnerabilities but does not filter live web traffic. Option C is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes logs for malicious or unauthorized activity, but it does not actively block web exploits inline. Option D is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch is a monitoring and observability service for metrics, logs, and alarms; it provides visibility but does not protect applications from SQL injection or XSS attacks the way AWS WAF does.

324.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 is an object storage service that delivers industry-leading performance, security, scalability, and data availability. It stores data as objects within buckets, scales virtually without limit, offers eleven nines of durability, and provides robust security and access controls, which accurately describes its core functionality.

Option A is incorrect because that description matches Amazon EBS, which provides high-performance block storage for use with EC2 instances, not S3. Option C is incorrect because a fully managed file storage system accessible over the SMB protocol describes Amazon FSx for Windows File Server, not S3. Option D is incorrect because a scalable, fully managed elastic NFS for use with AWS Cloud services and on-premises resources describes Amazon EFS. Only option B correctly characterizes Amazon S3 as scalable, secure object storage.

325.text:
Option C is correct because regularly rotating access keys is an IAM security best practice that limits the window of exposure if a key is compromised. By rotating long-term credentials on a recurring schedule and removing old keys, a company reduces the risk that leaked or stale keys can be used by attackers, thereby increasing security in the AWS Cloud.

Option A is incorrect because enabling programmatic access for all IAM users unnecessarily expands the credential attack surface and violates least privilege. Option B is incorrect because IAM roles, not IAM users, are the recommended way to delegate permissions, since roles provide temporary credentials and avoid sharing long-term keys. Option D is incorrect because customer managed policies are reusable, versioned, and easier to audit and maintain than inline policies, so preferring inline policies does not improve security. Rotating access keys is the action that strengthens the security posture.

326.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Inspector is an automated security assessment service that scans Amazon EC2 instances for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure. It uses assessment templates and findings based on known vulnerability databases (CVEs) and security best practices to evaluate instances, directly matching the requirement to check for vulnerabilities using predefined assessments.

Option A is incorrect because AWS WAF is a web application firewall that filters malicious HTTP/HTTPS requests; it does not assess instances for software vulnerabilities. Option B is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor offers broad best-practice recommendations across cost, performance, security, and fault tolerance, but it is not a vulnerability scanner using assessment templates for EC2. Option D is incorrect because AWS Shield protects against distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks and does not perform vulnerability assessments. Amazon Inspector is the purpose-built vulnerability assessment service.

327.text:
Option C is correct because under the AWS shared responsibility model, the customer is always responsible for their own data, including its classification, integrity, and management. AWS secures the underlying cloud infrastructure ("security of the cloud"), while the customer retains full ownership and control of the content they place in the cloud ("security in the cloud"), making customer data a constant customer responsibility.

Option A is incorrect because software license responsibility varies by service model; for many managed services AWS handles the underlying software, so licenses are not always the customer's responsibility. Option B is incorrect because networking responsibility is shared and depends on the service; AWS manages the physical and much of the virtual network infrastructure. Option D is incorrect because encryption keys can be managed by AWS through services such as AWS KMS or by the customer, so key management is not always solely the customer's responsibility. Customer data is the one item the customer always owns.

328.text:
Option C is correct because the Align phase of the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework's cloud transformation journey focuses on identifying capability gaps and demonstrating how cloud adoption accelerates desired business outcomes. It builds the case for change by aligning stakeholders and showing the value the cloud delivers toward business goals.

Option A is incorrect because the Scale phase focuses on expanding production workloads to realize value at greater scale after initial deployments succeed. Option B is incorrect because the Envision phase focuses on identifying and prioritizing transformation opportunities tied to strategic business objectives, rather than demonstrating accelerated outcomes. Option D is incorrect because the Launch phase focuses on delivering pilot initiatives into production and demonstrating incremental value from those specific projects. The Align phase is the one centered on demonstrating how the cloud helps accelerate business outcomes across the organization.

329.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Inspector provides automated, continuous vulnerability management that scans Amazon EC2 instances and container images for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure. It automatically reassesses resources as they change and reports findings, which directly meets the requirement for an automated, ongoing scan of EC2 instances for software vulnerabilities.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes logs and network activity for malicious behavior, not a software vulnerability scanner. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Detective helps investigate and analyze the root cause of security findings after detection; it does not scan instances for vulnerabilities. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Cognito provides user authentication, authorization, and identity management for applications and is unrelated to vulnerability scanning. Amazon Inspector is the service purpose-built for continuous EC2 vulnerability assessment.

330.text:
Options B and D are correct because two key advantages of moving to the AWS Cloud are the pay-as-you-go pricing model and the elimination of capacity guessing. With pay-as-you-go, a company pays only for the resources it actually consumes, avoiding large upfront capital expenditures. Because AWS resources scale on demand, companies no longer need to over-provision or guess future capacity needs, and they can scale up or down as requirements change.

Option A is incorrect because security is a shared responsibility; AWS secures the cloud infrastructure, but customers remain responsible for security in the cloud, so all security is never turned over to AWS. Option C is incorrect because moving to the cloud means AWS manages the physical infrastructure, so customers give up, rather than gain, full control over physical hardware. Option E is incorrect because managing user access controls, such as IAM permissions, remains the customer's responsibility in the cloud.

331.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Global Accelerator improves application availability and network performance by routing user traffic onto the AWS global network at the nearest edge location. It uses anycast static IP addresses and the optimized AWS backbone to send traffic to the closest healthy endpoint, reducing latency and jitter compared to traversing the public internet.

Option A is incorrect because a route table directs traffic within a VPC by mapping destinations to targets; it does not route traffic across the AWS worldwide network for performance gains. Option B is incorrect because AWS Transit Gateway connects multiple VPCs and on-premises networks through a central hub, simplifying connectivity but not specifically accelerating traffic over the global backbone. Option D is incorrect because Amazon VPC is the isolated virtual network in which resources run; it is foundational networking, not a global performance-acceleration feature. AWS Global Accelerator is purpose-built for this requirement.

332.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network (CDN) that caches and serves content from a worldwide network of edge locations close to end users. By deploying and serving application content from these edge locations, CloudFront reduces latency and improves performance for users regardless of their geographic location.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Auto Scaling adjusts the number of compute resources to match demand, but it does not place applications geographically closer to users. Option C is incorrect because AWS AppSync is a managed GraphQL service for building data-driven APIs; it does not provide edge-based content delivery to end users. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Route 53 is a DNS service that can route users to endpoints based on policies such as latency, but it resolves domain names rather than deploying and caching application content at the edge. CloudFront is the service designed to bring applications close to end users.

333.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Artifact is a self-service portal that provides on-demand access to AWS compliance reports and security documentation, such as SOC reports, PCI DSS attestations, and ISO certifications. It is the authoritative resource for users who need compliance-related information and audit reports about AWS.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Marketplace is a digital catalog for finding, buying, and deploying third-party software and services, not a repository of AWS compliance reports. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs automated vulnerability assessments of resources; it does not provide AWS compliance documentation. Option D is incorrect because AWS Support provides technical assistance, guidance, and troubleshooting through support plans, but the compliance artifacts themselves are retrieved from AWS Artifact. AWS Artifact is the dedicated source for compliance reports and agreements.

334.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) is a fully managed message queuing service that lets distributed application components send, store, and receive messages between each other reliably. By decoupling the ecommerce applications through queues, SQS enables them to exchange messages asynchronously without losing data, directly meeting the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Auto Scaling adjusts compute capacity to match demand and does not pass messages between applications. Option B is incorrect because Elastic Load Balancing distributes incoming traffic across multiple targets for availability and scaling; it does not provide inter-application messaging. Option D is incorrect because while Amazon Kinesis Data Streams handles real-time streaming data ingestion and processing, the requirement for general application-to-application messaging and decoupling is the classic use case for Amazon SQS, which is the simplest and most appropriate fit.

335.text:
Option D is correct because dividing a monolithic application into microservices is the embodiment of the Well-Architected best practice to implement loosely coupled dependencies. By breaking the application into independent services that communicate through well-defined interfaces, the company reduces the impact of failures, enables independent scaling, and improves maintainability, which addresses the scaling and maintenance problems described.

Option A is incorrect because integrating functional testing into deployment is a valuable practice but does not describe decomposing a monolith into microservices. Option B is incorrect because using automation to deploy changes addresses deployment consistency and operational excellence, not the architectural decoupling achieved by microservices. Option C is incorrect because deploying the application to multiple locations improves availability and reliability through redundancy, but it does not describe splitting the application into independent services. Implementing loosely coupled dependencies is the best practice directly reflected by the migration to microservices.

336.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon WorkSpaces is a fully managed Desktop-as-a-Service (DaaS) solution that provisions secure, persistent cloud-based virtual desktops for users. An administrator can use WorkSpaces to quickly deliver Windows or Linux desktop environments to many employees without managing on-premises desktop hardware, which meets the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Organizations centrally manages and governs multiple AWS accounts with consolidated billing and policies; it does not provide desktop environments. Option B is incorrect because AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for running containers, not virtual desktops. Option C is incorrect because AWS WAF is a web application firewall that protects web applications from common exploits and is unrelated to provisioning employee desktops. Amazon WorkSpaces is the purpose-built service for delivering managed virtual desktop environments to employees.

337.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a Platform as a Service (PaaS) that lets developers deploy applications quickly by simply uploading their code, while AWS automatically handles capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto scaling, and application health monitoring. This abstracts away the underlying infrastructure, directly minimizing the complexity of managing AWS resources and enabling rapid deployment.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Config records and evaluates resource configurations for compliance and auditing; it does not deploy applications. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EC2 provides raw virtual servers, which would require the company to manually configure the operating system, load balancing, scaling, and deployment, increasing rather than reducing management complexity. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Personalize is a machine learning service for building real-time recommendation systems and has nothing to do with general application deployment.

338.text:
Options B and C are correct because both services leverage the AWS global network of edge locations. Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network (CDN) that caches content at edge locations close to users to reduce latency. AWS Global Accelerator uses edge locations as entry points to the AWS global network, routing user traffic over the optimized AWS backbone to improve performance and availability for applications.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers that runs within Regions, not at edge locations.
