Option D is incorrect because AWS Wavelength embeds AWS compute and storage within telecommunications providers' 5G networks (Wavelength Zones), which are distinct from CloudFront edge locations. Option E is incorrect because Amazon VPC is a regional construct that provides an isolated virtual network within a Region and does not use edge locations.

339.text:
Option B is correct because AWS re:Post is a free, community-driven question-and-answer service that replaced the AWS Forums. It allows AWS users to join community groups, ask technical questions, find vetted answers, and read community-generated articles and best practices at no cost.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS Knowledge Center is a collection of official articles and videos answering common AWS support questions; it is curated by AWS rather than being a community group platform for asking and answering questions. Option C is incorrect because AWS IQ is a paid service that connects customers with third-party AWS Certified experts for hands-on project help. Option D is incorrect because AWS Enterprise Support is a paid premium support tier that provides a Technical Account Manager and fast response times, not a free community platform.

340.text:
Option D is correct because an Amazon CloudWatch alarm monitors a metric such as CPU utilization and, when a defined threshold is breached, can trigger an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling policy to add or remove instances. CloudWatch alarms are the standard mechanism that initiates dynamic scaling actions based on resource metrics.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon SQS is a message queuing service; while queue depth can be a scaling metric, SQS itself does not directly initiate scaling actions and is not used to track CPU utilization. Option B is incorrect because Amazon SNS is a pub/sub notification service used to send alerts and messages, not to trigger scaling based on metrics. Option C is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager provides operational management, patching, and automation for resources, but it is not the feature that triggers Auto Scaling based on CPU utilization thresholds.

341.text:
Option D is correct because the core benefit of AWS serverless computing, such as AWS Lambda or AWS Fargate, is that the management of the underlying infrastructure is offloaded to AWS. AWS handles provisioning, patching, scaling, and maintaining servers, allowing customers to focus on writing code rather than managing servers.

Option A is incorrect because application deployment and management are still required; developers must deploy their code and manage their application logic, even though infrastructure is abstracted. Option B is incorrect because, under the shared responsibility model, the customer remains responsible for application-level security such as IAM permissions and code, so security is not fully managed by AWS. Option C is incorrect because monitoring and logging are still needed and recommended; serverless applications still require observability through services like Amazon CloudWatch.

342.text:
Option B is correct because "Make frequent, small, reversible changes" is a recognized design principle of the operational excellence pillar of the AWS Well-Architected Framework. Small, reversible changes reduce the scope and impact of failures and make it easier to roll back if a change causes problems, improving the ability to operate and evolve workloads.

Option A is incorrect because "Go global in minutes" is a general benefit of cloud computing, not an operational excellence design principle. Option C is incorrect because implementing a strong identity and access management foundation is a design principle of the security pillar, not operational excellence. Option D is incorrect because "Stop spending money on running and maintaining data centers" describes a cost optimization concept and a general cloud value proposition rather than an operational excellence principle.

343.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 is object storage designed for durable, highly available storage of static content at low cost, offering 99.999999999% (eleven nines) durability. It provides virtually unlimited, infinitely scalable storage capacity without provisioning, making it ideal for static content at the lowest cost.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides block storage volumes attached to individual EC2 instances; volumes have fixed provisioned sizes and are not infinitely scalable or the lowest-cost option for static content. Option C is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway is a hybrid service that connects on-premises environments to AWS storage; it is not itself the scalable object store. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EFS is a managed shared file system that is generally more expensive than S3 and is designed for file-based workloads rather than lowest-cost static object storage.

344.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Lifecycle policies automate transitioning objects to lower-cost storage classes, such as S3 Standard-IA, S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval, or S3 Glacier Deep Archive, after a defined period. This automatically archives data that is no longer frequently accessed, reducing storage costs without manual intervention.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Versioning preserves multiple versions of an object to protect against accidental deletion or overwrites; it does not move data to cheaper archive tiers. Option C is incorrect because S3 Object Lock enforces write-once-read-many (WORM) retention for compliance, preventing deletion rather than reducing cost through archiving. Option D is incorrect because S3 Inventory generates reports listing objects and their metadata for auditing and management; it provides visibility but does not itself archive data or save storage costs.

345.text:
Options A and C are correct because both establish connectivity between on-premises networks and an Amazon VPC. AWS VPN (Site-to-Site VPN) creates an encrypted IPsec tunnel over the public internet between an on-premises network and a VPC. AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated, private physical network connection from on-premises to AWS, providing consistent, high-bandwidth, low-latency connectivity to a VPC.

Option B is incorrect because Elastic Load Balancing distributes incoming application traffic across multiple targets within AWS; it does not connect on-premises networks. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering connects two VPCs together, not an on-premises network to a VPC. Option E is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network for caching and delivering content to users, not a hybrid network connectivity service.

346.text:
Options B and D are correct because they are recognized benefits of cloud computing. "Deploy globally in minutes" reflects the ability to launch applications across multiple AWS Regions worldwide quickly to reduce latency and improve customer experience. "Take advantage of economies of scale" reflects that AWS aggregates usage from many customers, achieving higher purchasing power and passing lower pay-as-you-go pricing on to customers.

Option A is incorrect because the benefit is the reverse: customers trade capital expenses (large upfront hardware purchases) for variable expenses (pay-as-you-go), not the other way around. Option C is incorrect because a key cloud benefit is the ability to stop guessing capacity and scale on demand, eliminating the need to plan capacity far in advance. Option E is incorrect because reducing dependencies on network connectivity is not a stated benefit of the AWS Cloud, which actually relies on network connectivity to access services.

347.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Timestream is a fully managed, purpose-built time-series database service designed to store and analyze trillions of time-stamped events per day. It is optimized for IoT, operational metrics, and application monitoring data, automatically scaling and tiering data between memory and cost-optimized storage.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Neptune is a graph database for highly connected data and relationships, not a time-series workload. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Forecast is a machine learning service that produces forecasts from historical data; it is an analytics/ML service rather than a database for storing trillions of events. Option D is incorrect because Amazon DocumentDB is a managed document database with MongoDB compatibility for JSON document workloads, not a time-series database service.

348.text:
Options D and E are correct because, under the AWS shared responsibility model, AWS is responsible for the security and operation "of the cloud." For a managed service like Amazon DynamoDB, AWS manages the infrastructure layer and operating system (E) and provides the public service endpoints used to store and retrieve data (D), along with patching and maintaining the underlying hardware and software.

Option A is incorrect because classifying data is always the customer's responsibility, as the customer controls and understands their own data. Option B is incorrect because configuring access permissions through IAM is the customer's responsibility for security "in the cloud." Option C is incorrect because managing encryption options, such as choosing and configuring encryption keys, is the customer's responsibility under the shared responsibility model.

349.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudTrail records API calls and account activity across AWS services, capturing details such as the identity of the caller, the time, the source IP, and the request parameters. This provides governance, compliance, and operational and risk auditing of actions taken in an AWS account.

Option A is incorrect because generating an IAM credentials report is a function of IAM, not CloudTrail. Option C is incorrect because assessing the compliance of resource configurations against rules and policies is the function of AWS Config, which evaluates configuration states over time. Option D is incorrect because ensuring EC2 instances are patched with the latest security updates is handled by AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager, not by CloudTrail.

350.text:
Option B is correct because a NAT gateway is an AWS managed service that allows instances in a private subnet to initiate outbound connections to the internet, such as pulling operating system updates, while preventing unsolicited inbound connections from the internet to those instances. This provides one-way outbound internet access without exposing the EC2 instance.

Option A is incorrect because a VPC endpoint provides private connectivity to specific AWS services without using the internet; it does not provide general outbound internet access for OS updates. Option C is incorrect because AWS PrivateLink enables private access to services and endpoints, not general outbound internet connectivity. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering connects two VPCs to each other privately and does not provide internet access for downloading updates.

351.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Security Token Service (AWS STS) issues temporary, limited-privilege security credentials for IAM users or federated users. These short-lived credentials are commonly used when assuming IAM roles, providing access to AWS services without long-term credentials and reducing the risk associated with persistent access keys.

Option A is incorrect because IAM policies define permissions (what actions are allowed on which resources); they do not generate temporary credentials. Option B is incorrect because IAM user groups are a way to organize users and attach common permissions, not a mechanism for issuing temporary credentials. Option D is incorrect because while AWS IAM Identity Center provides single sign-on and federated access (and uses STS under the hood), the specific service whose purpose is to provide temporary security credentials is AWS STS.

352.text:
Option A is correct because the IAM credential report is a downloadable CSV file that lists all IAM users in the account along with the status of their various credentials, including passwords, access keys (and rotation status), and whether MFA devices are enabled. This directly meets the requirement to generate a user list and view credential status.

Option B is incorrect because AWS IAM Identity Center manages single sign-on and federated workforce access across accounts; it does not generate the per-user credential status report. Option C is incorrect because IAM Access Analyzer identifies resources shared with external entities to find unintended access, not the status of user credentials. Option D is incorrect because the AWS Cost and Usage Report provides detailed billing and usage data, which is unrelated to IAM credential status.

353.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Comprehend is a natural language processing (NLP) service that uses machine learning to find insights and relationships in text. It can automatically classify documents and detect topics, sentiment, entities, and key phrases, making it ideal for automatically classifying electronic books based on their content.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a data warehouse for analytical SQL queries on structured data, not a text classification service. Option B is incorrect because Amazon CloudSearch provides search functionality over a collection of data; it indexes documents for searching rather than classifying them by content using ML. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Aurora is a managed relational database engine and provides no built-in text analysis or content classification capabilities.

354.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Redshift is a fully managed, petabyte-scale cloud data warehouse designed for analytics. AWS handles the provisioning, patching, backups, and scaling of the underlying hardware and software, removing the need for manual hardware and software management while supporting large-scale analytical workloads.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon DocumentDB is a managed document (JSON) database for operational workloads, not a petabyte-scale analytical data warehouse. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Neptune is a managed graph database for connected data and relationships, not a data warehouse for analytics. Option D is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache provides in-memory caching with Redis or Memcached to accelerate application performance; it is not a data warehouse solution for analyzing petabytes of data.

355.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Cost Explorer provides EC2 rightsizing recommendations at no additional cost. It analyzes historical usage and performance to identify idle and underutilized instances and suggests optimal instance sizes or termination, helping customers reduce costs without extra charges.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS Well-Architected Tool reviews workloads against best-practice pillars and provides architectural guidance, not EC2 rightsizing recommendations. Option B is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch collects metrics, logs, and alarms for monitoring; while its data informs decisions, it does not by itself produce rightsizing recommendations. Option D is incorrect because Amazon S3 analytics provides storage class analysis to optimize S3 storage costs, not EC2 instance rightsizing.

356.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Software Development Kit (SDK) provides language-specific libraries (for languages such as Python, Java, JavaScript, and others) that let developers call AWS service APIs directly from their application code. The SDK handles authentication, request signing, and serialization, making programmatic access to AWS services straightforward.

Option B is incorrect because the AWS Management Console is a web-based graphical interface for humans to manage resources, not a mechanism for application code to access services. Option C is incorrect because AWS CodePipeline is a continuous delivery service that automates release pipelines, not a library for calling AWS services from code. Option D is incorrect because AWS Config tracks and evaluates resource configurations for compliance; it does not provide programmatic access to AWS services from application code.

357.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server is a fully managed file storage service built on Windows Server that natively supports the Server Message Block (SMB) protocol, NTFS, and Windows features such as Active Directory integration and Distributed File System (DFS). This makes it ideal for Microsoft-based workloads requiring SMB access.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EFS is a managed file system that uses the NFS protocol for Linux-based workloads, not SMB or native Windows file systems. Option B is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Lustre is a high-performance file system designed for compute-intensive workloads like HPC and machine learning, not Microsoft/SMB file serving. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides block storage volumes attached to a single EC2 instance and is not a shared, fully managed Windows file server.

358.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) is purpose-built to migrate on-premises databases to managed AWS database services with a simplified process and minimal downtime. It supports both homogeneous and heterogeneous migrations and can keep the source database fully operational during the migration through continuous data replication.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway provides hybrid storage integration between on-premises environments and AWS storage, not database migration. Option B is incorrect because AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) is designed to lift and shift entire servers and applications to EC2, not specifically to migrate databases to managed database services. Option C is incorrect because AWS DataSync automates transferring file and object data between storage systems, not migrating databases into managed database engines.

359.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Neptune is a fully managed graph database service optimized for storing and navigating highly connected data and relationships. It supports popular graph models and query languages such as Property Graph (Gremlin) and W3C RDF (SPARQL), making it ideal for use cases like social networks, recommendation engines, and fraud detection.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Aurora is a managed relational database compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL, not a graph database. Option B is incorrect because Amazon FSx is a managed file storage service, not a database. Option C is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a managed NoSQL key-value and document database; while highly scalable, it is not a purpose-built graph database for relationship traversal.

360.text:
Option C is correct because AWS DataSync is a data transfer service that automates and accelerates moving large amounts of unstructured data between on-premises storage and AWS. It encrypts data in transit (inflight) using TLS and performs end-to-end data integrity validation during the transfer, directly meeting the security and validation requirements.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Application Migration Service performs lift-and-shift migration of entire servers to EC2, not the transfer of unstructured data with integrity validation. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EFS is a managed file storage service, a destination for files, not a migration/transfer service that moves data with inflight encryption and validation. Option D is incorrect because AWS Migration Hub provides centralized tracking and visibility of migration progress across tools; it does not itself transfer or validate data.

361.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI) is a unified tool that lets developers control and provision AWS services directly from the command line and within scripts. It enables programmatic, repeatable automation of resource provisioning and management, which is exactly what is needed to quickly provision and manage services via scripts.

Option B is incorrect because AWS CodeBuild is a managed continuous integration service that compiles source code and runs tests; it produces build artifacts rather than provisioning arbitrary AWS services on demand. Option C is incorrect because the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) is guidance and best-practice documentation for organizing a cloud migration, not a scripting or provisioning tool. Option D is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides secure interactive shell or port-forwarding access to managed instances; it is for connecting to instances, not for scripting the provisioning of AWS services.

362.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Glue is a fully managed, serverless extract, transform, and load (ETL) service designed specifically for discovering, cataloging, cleaning, and transforming data so it can be moved between data stores. It eliminates infrastructure management and integrates natively with sources like Amazon S3, making it the purpose-built ETL service.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Athena is an interactive query service that runs SQL against data in S3; it analyzes data rather than performing ETL pipelines. Option C is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage that holds data but does not transform it. Option D is incorrect because AWS Snowball Edge is a physical data transfer and edge computing device used to move large volumes of data into AWS, not to perform ETL transformations.

363.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) provides best-practice guidance organized into perspectives that help organizations identify and prioritize business transformation opportunities and evaluate their readiness for cloud adoption. It is the AWS tool specifically aimed at assessing organizational cloud readiness and planning the transformation journey.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Managed Services (AMS) operates and manages AWS infrastructure on a customer's behalf; it is an operational offering, not a readiness-assessment framework. Option C is incorrect because the AWS Well-Architected Framework evaluates the technical design of specific workloads against six pillars, rather than assessing overall organizational transformation readiness. Option D is incorrect because AWS Migration Hub tracks the progress of application migrations across tools; it monitors migration execution rather than evaluating cloud readiness and business transformation opportunities.

364.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Application Discovery Service is designed to collect detailed configuration and usage data about on-premises servers, including hostnames, IP addresses, MAC addresses, and resource utilization. This inventory information helps plan and prioritize a migration to the AWS Cloud, which is exactly what the company requires.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DataSync moves and synchronizes file and object data between on-premises storage and AWS; it transfers data rather than discovering server configuration details. Option B is incorrect because AWS Application Migration Service performs the actual lift-and-shift rehosting of servers, not the discovery and inventory of their attributes. Option D is incorrect because AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) migrates databases between sources and targets and does not gather host-level network details like MAC addresses.

365.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Identity and Access Management Access Analyzer (specifically its last-accessed and service-usage information features) can report which services a user or role has accessed and when, helping administrators review activity within a specified date range and right-size permissions. It is the IAM capability focused on analyzing access to AWS resources.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 access control lists (ACLs) define object and bucket permissions; they grant access rather than report a history of services used. Option B is incorrect because AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) provisions and manages SSL/TLS certificates and has nothing to do with tracking user service usage. Option C is incorrect because Network Access Analyzer evaluates network reachability and pathways within a VPC, not which AWS services a user has accessed.

366.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Lightsail provides easy-to-launch virtual private servers and pre-configured application stacks (such as WordPress, LAMP, and Node.js) with bundled compute, storage, and networking at a predictable price. This makes it the ideal choice for running a workload using pre-configured instances with minimal setup.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon Athena is a serverless query service for analyzing data in S3 and does not provide application-stack instances. Option C is incorrect because AWS Outposts extends AWS infrastructure into on-premises data centers; it is for hybrid hardware deployments, not simple pre-configured application stacks. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EC2 provides raw, unconfigured virtual machines that require the user to install and configure the application stack manually, rather than offering ready-to-use pre-configured stacks.

367.text:
Option B is correct because the Threat detection capability within the AWS CAF security perspective focuses on understanding and identifying potential security misconfigurations, threats, or unexpected behaviors in the environment so the organization can prioritize the protective controls it needs. This directly matches the company's goal of surfacing misconfigurations and prioritizing protections.

Option A is incorrect because Identity and access management governs who can access resources and under what conditions; it manages permissions rather than detecting misconfigurations or unexpected behavior. Option C is incorrect because Platform engineering builds compliant, repeatable cloud platforms and tooling, not threat identification. Option D is incorrect because Availability and continuity management focuses on resilience, backups, and recovery to keep workloads running, which is unrelated to identifying security misconfigurations and prioritizing protective controls.

368.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon RDS for MySQL is a managed relational database service in which AWS handles the underlying hardware provisioning, patching, automated backups, high availability through Multi-AZ deployments, and read-replica replication. This relieves the user of managing hardware, resiliency, and replication while still providing a relational MySQL database.

Option A is incorrect because running MySQL on Amazon ECS still requires the customer to manage container configuration, database resiliency, and replication. Option B is incorrect because running MySQL on Amazon EC2 leaves the customer fully responsible for the operating system, patching, backups, and replication. Option D is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis is an in-memory caching data store, not a relational database, and does not meet the requirement for a relational database.

369.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CloudTrail records API calls and account activity across AWS services, capturing who made a request, when, and what action was taken. It would log the API call (such as a terminate-instance action) that made the EC2 instance inaccessible or removed it from the console, providing the audit trail needed to determine the cause.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch Logs collects and stores application and system log data and operational metrics; it does not record the AWS API actions that change resource state. Option B is incorrect because AWS Security Hub aggregates and prioritizes security findings but does not provide the granular API-call history needed to identify a specific action. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs automated vulnerability assessments of workloads, not auditing of management actions taken against an instance.

370.text:
Option D is correct because under the AWS shared responsibility model, AWS is responsible for security of the cloud, which includes the physical and environmental controls of the data centers, such as facility security, power, cooling, and hardware. These foundational infrastructure protections are entirely AWS's responsibility.

Option A is incorrect because security awareness and training for personnel is a customer responsibility related to their own organization and users. Option B is incorrect because developing an IAM password policy is configuration the customer controls within their account, falling under security in the cloud. Option C is incorrect because patching of the guest operating system on services like EC2 is the customer's responsibility, since the customer manages the OS layer of their instances.

371.text:
Option C is correct because agility refers to the ability to quickly provision and access compute, storage, database, and other infrastructure resources in minutes, allowing teams to experiment and innovate faster without lengthy procurement cycles. This describes the rapid deployment benefit the question highlights.

Option A is incorrect because elasticity is the ability to automatically scale resources up or down to match demand, not specifically the speed of initial provisioning. Option B is incorrect because cost savings refers to reducing expenditure by paying only for what you use and avoiding upfront capital costs, which is unrelated to deployment speed. Option D is incorrect because reliability is the ability of a system to recover from failures and consistently perform as expected, not the rapid provisioning of new resources.

372.text:
Option B is correct because under the AWS shared responsibility model, the customer is responsible for managing their IAM users, credentials, and access keys, including rotating those access and secret keys regularly as a security best practice. Identity and credential management falls under the customer's security in the cloud responsibilities.

Option A is incorrect because access and secret keys should be rotated regularly to reduce the risk of compromise; treating them as static is a poor security practice. Option C is incorrect because AWS does not automatically rotate customer IAM user access keys; that responsibility rests with the customer. Option D is incorrect because the AWS Support team does not rotate customer keys; key management is controlled by the customer within their own account.

373.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Storage Gateway, through its Tape Gateway configuration, presents a virtual tape library (VTL) interface to existing backup applications and stores virtual tapes durably in Amazon S3 and Amazon S3 Glacier. This lets the company extend its physical tape library into the AWS Cloud without changing its backup workflows.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EFS provides a managed NFS file system for Linux workloads and does not present a tape interface to backup software. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides block storage volumes attached to EC2 instances, not a tape library extension. Option C is incorrect because while Amazon S3 ultimately stores the data, S3 alone does not provide the virtual tape library interface that backup applications expect; Storage Gateway is required to bridge the on-premises tape workflow to S3.

374.text:
Option D is correct because under the AWS shared responsibility model, the customer is responsible for security in the cloud, which includes applying updates and security patches to the guest operating system and applications running on their Amazon EC2 instances. Maintaining the EC2 OS and software is the customer's task.

Option A is incorrect because physical connectivity among Availability Zones is part of the underlying infrastructure that AWS owns and maintains. Option B is incorrect because network switch maintenance is hardware infrastructure managed entirely by AWS. Option C is incorrect because hardware updates and firmware patches for the physical servers and devices are AWS's responsibility under security of the cloud, not the customer's.

375.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Route 53 is a highly available and scalable Domain Name System (DNS) web service that translates domain names into IP addresses and routes end users to applications. With features like health checks and latency-based and geolocation routing, it reliably connects global users to the ecommerce platform.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EC2 provides compute capacity (virtual servers) and is not a DNS service. Option B is incorrect because Amazon VPC provides an isolated virtual network for AWS resources but does not perform global DNS resolution. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS is a managed relational database service and has no role in DNS routing of users to the platform.

376.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EC2 gives the customer full control over the guest operating system, including the responsibility and ability to manage patching, updates, and configuration of that OS. This makes EC2 the right choice when a company must retain complete control of patch management for the operating systems hosting its applications.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL database where AWS handles the underlying servers and OS, leaving no guest OS for the customer to patch. Option C is incorrect because AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service in which AWS manages the runtime environment and patching, so the customer cannot control the OS. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS is a managed database service where AWS handles the underlying operating system and database patching, removing customer control over the guest OS.

377.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EC2 follows the shared responsibility model in which the customer manages the guest operating system, meaning the customer must apply OS updates and security patches to their instances. EC2 is therefore the service that requires the company to patch the guest operating system.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL database where AWS maintains the underlying infrastructure and operating system. Option B is incorrect because Amazon S3 is fully managed object storage with no customer-accessible operating system to patch. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Aurora is a managed relational database service in which AWS handles the operating system and database engine patching, not the customer.

378.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CloudShell is a browser-based, pre-authenticated shell that launches directly from the AWS Management Console, automatically using the signed-in user's credentials. It provides a ready-to-use command-line environment with the AWS CLI preinstalled, requiring no separate setup or authentication.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS API is the programmatic interface to AWS services, not a browser-based pre-authenticated console feature. Option B is incorrect because AWS Lightsail provides virtual private servers and application stacks, not an in-console pre-authenticated shell. Option C is incorrect because while AWS Cloud9 is a browser-based IDE, it is a separate environment that requires provisioning an underlying compute environment and is not the pre-authenticated shell launched directly from the console that the question describes.

379.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Enterprise Support provides the highest level of support, including access to a Technical Account Manager and a Support Concierge team that assists with billing and account-level questions. The concierge service is an Enterprise Support benefit aimed at handling non-technical account and billing inquiries quickly.

Option A is incorrect because Basic Support offers only documentation, whitepapers, and access to the support forums, with no concierge service. Option B is incorrect because Developer Support provides business-hours email access to support engineers but does not include a support concierge. Option C is incorrect because Business Support offers 24/7 technical support and full Trusted Advisor checks but still does not include the support concierge, which is reserved for Enterprise Support.

380.text:
Option C is correct because AWS IAM Access Analyzer continuously analyzes resource policies to identify resources such as S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, and Lambda functions that are shared with external entities outside your account or organization. It generates findings highlighting unintended external access, exactly matching the requirement to search for externally shared resources.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon OpenSearch Service is a managed search and analytics engine for log and document data, not a tool for detecting external resource sharing. Option B is incorrect because AWS Control Tower sets up and governs a secure multi-account environment but does not specifically identify individual externally shared resources. Option D is incorrect because AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers and has no role in analyzing resource-sharing access.

381.text:
Option C is correct because VPC Flow Logs capture information about the inbound and outbound IP traffic going to and from network interfaces within a VPC. This data can be published to Amazon CloudWatch Logs or S3 and is used to monitor traffic, troubleshoot connectivity, and detect anomalous network activity.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch Logs is a destination that stores log data; on its own it does not generate the network interface traffic records that VPC Flow Logs produce. Option B is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail logs AWS API and management activity, not packet-level network traffic on interfaces. Option D is incorrect because AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) controls authentication and authorization to AWS resources and does not record network traffic flows.

382.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon CloudFront is a global content delivery network (CDN) that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to users worldwide with low latency and high transfer speeds. It caches content at edge locations close to users and integrates with services like AWS Shield and AWS WAF for secure delivery.

Option B is incorrect because Elastic Load Balancing distributes incoming traffic across targets within a region; it is not a global content delivery network. Option C is incorrect because Amazon S3 provides object storage and, while it can serve as a CloudFront origin, it does not itself provide global edge caching and low-latency delivery. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Elastic Transcoder converts media files between formats and is not a content delivery network.

383.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) is purpose-built to migrate databases to AWS quickly and securely, including moving an on-premises PostgreSQL database into Amazon RDS. It supports both homogeneous migrations and keeps the source database operational during the migration, minimizing downtime.

Option A is incorrect because the Cloud Adoption Readiness Tool is a self-assessment questionnaire that gauges organizational readiness for the cloud, not a database migration tool. Option B is incorrect because AWS Migration Hub tracks and provides visibility into migration progress across tools but does not itself perform the database migration. Option D is incorrect because AWS Application Migration Service performs lift-and-shift rehosting of entire servers rather than migrating individual databases into a managed service like Amazon RDS.

384.text:
Option D is correct because the AWS Partner Network (APN) is a global community of third-party consulting and technology partners who can help customers maintain, support, build, and operate their AWS environments to meet business needs. Engaging APN consulting partners is the appropriate way to bring in qualified third-party expertise.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Support provides technical and account assistance directly from AWS, not third-party consultants. Option B is incorrect because AWS Organizations is used to centrally govern and consolidate billing across multiple AWS accounts, not to engage outside consultants. Option C is incorrect because AWS Service Catalog lets organizations create and manage approved catalogs of IT products for internal use, which is unrelated to hiring third-party consultants.

385.text:
Option A is correct because EC2 Image Builder is a fully managed AWS service that automates the creation, testing, and management of customized, secure, and up-to-date server images (AMIs and container images). It lets you define a pipeline that builds an image, applies hardening and patches, runs validation tests, and distributes the resulting image across Regions, removing the manual effort of building and maintaining custom images.

Option B is incorrect because an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) is the image artifact itself, a template used to launch instances, not a service that creates, tests, or manages images. Option C is incorrect because AWS Launch Wizard guides the sizing and deployment of specific third-party applications such as SAP or SQL Server on AWS, not the building of custom EC2 images. Option D is incorrect because AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a platform-as-a-service for deploying and scaling web applications and handles provisioning and capacity, not the creation and lifecycle management of custom AMIs.

386.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Organizations lets a company centrally manage and govern multiple AWS accounts. It supports automated account creation, the application of service control policies (SCPs) to enforce permission guardrails across organizational units, and consolidated billing that aggregates usage from all member accounts into a single payer for simplified payment and volume discounts. This directly meets all three stated requirements.

Option B is incorrect because Cost Explorer is a visualization and reporting tool for analyzing cost and usage trends; it does not create accounts or apply SCPs. Option C is incorrect because AWS Budgets sets spending thresholds and sends alerts, providing no account governance or policy enforcement. Option D is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor offers best-practice recommendations across cost, performance, security, and fault tolerance, but it cannot automate account creation, apply SCPs, or manage billing.

387.text:
Option B is correct because IAM roles provide temporary security credentials that applications running on Amazon EC2 instances can assume to access other AWS services such as Amazon SNS. By attaching an instance profile with an IAM role, the application receives automatically rotated credentials without hardcoding access keys, granting permission to call SNS APIs securely and following AWS best practices.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) provisions and manages SSL/TLS certificates for encryption in transit; it does not grant service permissions. Option C is incorrect because AWS Security Hub aggregates and prioritizes security findings across accounts; it is a monitoring and posture service, not a mechanism for granting access. Option D is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes activity for malicious behavior; it does not authorize applications to call AWS services.

388.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Snowball Edge is a rugged edge computing and data transfer device designed for environments with intermittent or no connectivity, such as ships at sea. It can collect, store, format, and process data locally using onboard compute, then physically ship the device back to AWS for ingestion, exactly matching the requirement to process data offline and move it to AWS later.

Option A is incorrect because AWS IoT Core requires reliable internet connectivity to ingest device telemetry into the cloud in near real time, which is unavailable at sea. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Lightsail provides simplified cloud virtual servers in AWS Regions and offers no disconnected edge capability. Option C is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway connects on-premises environments to AWS cloud storage and depends on a network connection to function, so it cannot operate during prolonged offline periods.

389.text:
Options B and E are correct because both Amazon RDS and Amazon Aurora are fully managed relational database services that support PostgreSQL compatibility. Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL runs the standard PostgreSQL engine with AWS handling patching, backups, and failover, while Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL-Compatible Edition is a cloud-native engine that is wire-compatible with PostgreSQL and delivers higher performance and durability, making both valid managed targets for the migration.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Athena is a serverless interactive query service for analyzing data in Amazon S3 using SQL, not a managed relational database. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EC2 would require the company to self-manage the PostgreSQL installation, patching, and backups, which is not a managed service. Option D is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value and document database and is not PostgreSQL compatible.

390.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Batch is a fully managed service that lets you plan, schedule, and run hundreds of thousands of batch computing jobs on AWS. It dynamically provisions the optimal type and quantity of compute resources based on the volume and resource requirements of the submitted jobs, handling job queuing, dependencies, and scaling without requiring you to manage cluster infrastructure.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Step Functions orchestrates workflows by coordinating multiple AWS services into serverless state machines; it is not designed to schedule and run large volumes of compute jobs. Option B is incorrect because AWS Service Catalog lets organizations create and manage catalogs of approved IT products for deployment, unrelated to batch job execution. Option C is incorrect because Amazon SQS is a managed message queuing service for decoupling components; it stores and delivers messages but does not provision compute or run the jobs themselves.

391.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides fully managed, highly reliable, and scalable file storage that is natively accessible over the Server Message Block (SMB) protocol. It is built on Windows Server, supports Active Directory integration and Windows NTFS features, and offers multi-AZ deployment options for high availability, directly meeting the SMB requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage accessed through APIs and HTTP, not the SMB file protocol. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EFS provides file storage using the NFS protocol for Linux-based workloads, not SMB. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides block-level storage volumes attached to a single EC2 instance and is not a shared file system accessible over SMB.

392.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Application Composer provides a visual canvas for designing and building serverless applications. You drag and drop AWS services such as Lambda functions, API Gateway, and DynamoDB onto the canvas, and the service automatically generates deployment-ready infrastructure as code (AWS SAM or CloudFormation templates), simplifying the architecture and authoring of serverless solutions.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Lambda is the serverless compute service that runs your function code; it does not provide a visual design tool for composing whole applications. Option B is incorrect because AWS Batch runs batch computing jobs and has no visual application design capability. Option D is incorrect because AWS App Runner is a service for deploying containerized web applications and APIs directly from source or images, not a visual builder for serverless architectures.

393.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Elastic Beanstalk lets a user with limited AWS knowledge quickly deploy and scale a Node.js web application. You simply upload your code, and Elastic Beanstalk automatically handles capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto scaling, and application health monitoring, while still giving access to the underlying resources if needed. This makes it the fastest path to a scalable deployment without deep AWS expertise.

Option A is incorrect because AWS CloudFormation requires authoring infrastructure-as-code templates, which demands more AWS knowledge than a beginner has. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EC2 requires manually provisioning, configuring, scaling, and maintaining the servers and runtime, increasing complexity and operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because AWS OpsWorks is a configuration management service using Chef or Puppet that requires familiarity with those tools, making it less suitable for a user who wants a quick, simple deployment.

394.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Shield is the managed service that protects applications from distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks. AWS Shield Standard is automatically enabled and provides always-on detection and automatic inline mitigations against common network and transport layer attacks, while Shield Advanced adds enhanced protections, exactly matching the description of always-on detection with automatic inline mitigation.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Inspector is an automated vulnerability management service that scans workloads for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure, not DDoS protection. Option B is incorrect because AWS WAF filters web requests against rules to block exploits such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting; it is layer 7 filtering rather than a dedicated always-on DDoS mitigation service. Option C is incorrect because Elastic Load Balancing distributes incoming traffic across targets and is not a DDoS protection service.

395.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Firewall Manager is a security management service that lets a company centrally configure and manage security rules, including VPC security groups, across multiple accounts in an AWS Organizations organization. It enforces a consistent baseline, automatically applies policies to existing and new accounts and resources, and audits for noncompliant configurations from a single place.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that monitors for malicious activity; it does not configure or manage security groups. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Detective analyzes and visualizes security data to investigate the root cause of findings, providing no management of security groups. Option D is incorrect because AWS WAF protects web applications by filtering HTTP/HTTPS requests at layer 7 and does not centrally manage VPC security groups across accounts.

396.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Marketplace is a curated digital catalog where customers can find, buy, deploy, and manage third-party software and services that run on AWS, including security software offered as a service by independent vendors. It provides flexible pricing and consolidated AWS billing, allowing the company to purchase the same vendor's security solution and deploy it directly into its AWS environment.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Partner Solutions Finder helps customers discover AWS Partners and their offerings but is not the transactional store for purchasing and deploying software. Option B is incorrect because AWS Support Center is where customers manage support cases and access technical assistance, not a software store. Option C is incorrect because the AWS Management Console is the web interface used to operate AWS resources; it is not where third-party security software is purchased.

397.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Auto Scaling, through an EC2 Auto Scaling group, performs health checks on instances and automatically terminates and replaces any instance that becomes impaired or fails a health check. This maintains the desired capacity of the fleet without manual intervention, ensuring unhealthy instances are continuously replaced with new, healthy ones.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon ECS orchestrates Docker containers and manages container tasks, not the health-based replacement of standalone EC2 instances. Option B is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty detects threats by analyzing activity logs; it does not replace failed instances. Option C is incorrect because AWS Shield protects against DDoS attacks and has no role in maintaining or replacing EC2 instances based on health.

398.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CloudFormation lets companies create and provision infrastructure from code by defining resources in declarative JSON or YAML templates. CloudFormation treats infrastructure as code, automatically provisioning and configuring the described resources as a managed stack, enabling repeatable, version-controlled, and automated deployments across environments.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EKS is a managed Kubernetes service for running containerized applications, not a tool for defining and provisioning AWS infrastructure from templates. Option B is incorrect because AWS Outposts extends AWS infrastructure and services to on-premises facilities; it is physical hardware, not an infrastructure-as-code service. Option C is incorrect because AWS CodePipeline is a continuous delivery service that automates release pipelines; it can deploy CloudFormation templates but does not itself define infrastructure as code.

399.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Route 53 is AWS's highly available and scalable Domain Name System (DNS) web service. It allows the company to register domain names and host the DNS records that route end users to the website, fulfilling the requirement to host the domain name for the migrated public website on AWS.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Lambda runs event-driven code without provisioning servers and does not provide domain registration or DNS hosting. Option C is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network that caches and accelerates content delivery from edge locations; it relies on DNS but does not host domain names. Option D is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated private network connection between on-premises and AWS and is unrelated to hosting a domain name.

400.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon RDS with multiple Availability Zones (Multi-AZ) provides high availability and fault tolerance for a PostgreSQL database. RDS synchronously replicates data to a standby instance in a separate Availability Zone and automatically fails over to that standby if the primary fails, ensuring the database remains available despite an AZ-level disruption.

Option A is incorrect because RDS in a single Availability Zone has no standby, so an AZ failure makes the database unavailable, providing neither high availability nor fault tolerance. Option B is incorrect because RDS snapshots are point-in-time backups used for restoration; they do not provide automatic failover or continuous availability. Option D is incorrect because AWS DMS is a service for migrating and replicating databases, not a configuration that makes an RDS database highly available and fault tolerant.

401.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudTrail records API calls and account activity across an AWS account, capturing details such as who made a request, when it occurred, and the affected resource. The TerminateInstances API call is logged in CloudTrail, allowing administrators to identify exactly when an Amazon EC2 instance was terminated and by whom for auditing and governance.

Option A is incorrect because AWS IAM manages users, roles, and permissions; it controls access but does not record when resources are terminated. Option C is incorrect because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes utilization to recommend rightsized resources, not a history of API actions. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EventBridge can react to events in near real time by routing them to targets, but it is an event bus for triggering actions rather than the auditable record of API history; CloudTrail is the service that identifies when the termination occurred.

402.text:
Options A and D are correct because both provide secure ways to connect to a Linux EC2 instance. Amazon EC2 Instance Connect lets administrators establish a browser-based or CLI SSH session to Amazon Linux 2 instances by pushing a temporary public key, removing the need to manage long-lived keys. AWS Systems Manager Session Manager opens an interactive shell session through the SSM agent without requiring open inbound ports, bastion hosts, or SSH keys, providing secure, auditable access.

Option B is incorrect because Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) is used to connect to Windows instances, not a Linux Amazon Linux 2 instance. Option C is incorrect because AWS Batch runs batch computing jobs and provides no mechanism to connect to an instance. Option E is incorrect because Amazon Connect is a cloud contact center service for customer engagement and has nothing to do with connecting to EC2 instances.

403.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up moving files over long distances between a client and an Amazon S3 bucket. It routes uploads and downloads through Amazon CloudFront's globally distributed edge locations, then transfers data to the bucket over AWS's optimized backbone network, significantly improving transfer speed for geographically distant clients while keeping the transfer secure.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Versioning preserves multiple versions of objects to protect against accidental deletion or overwrite; it does not accelerate transfers. Option C is incorrect because S3 ACLs are a legacy access control mechanism for managing permissions, unrelated to transfer speed. Option D is incorrect because S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers to optimize storage cost based on usage patterns, not transfer performance.

404.text:
Option C is correct because the AWS account root user is the single sign-in identity created automatically when an AWS account is first established, using the email address and password provided at sign-up. It has complete, unrestricted access to all account resources, which is why AWS recommends securing it with MFA and using IAM identities for everyday tasks.

Option A is incorrect because MFA can be enabled for both the root user and IAM users; the root user is not the only identity that can use MFA. Option B is incorrect because IAM users and federated identities can also access the AWS Management Console, so the root user is not the only one able to do so. Option D is incorrect because the root user's password can be changed at any time through account settings.

405.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes historical utilization metrics, such as CPU, memory, and network usage, using machine learning to recommend optimal, rightsized Amazon EC2 instance types and sizes. These recommendations help reduce cost and improve performance by matching instances to actual workload demand based on observed usage data.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS Pricing Calculator estimates the cost of AWS services based on configurations you specify; it does not analyze historical workload data to recommend rightsizing. Option C is incorrect because AWS App Runner deploys and runs containerized web applications and has no rightsizing recommendation function. Option D is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager provides operational management and automation for resources, but it is not the dedicated service that delivers rightsizing recommendations from historical usage; Compute Optimizer fills that role.

406.text:
Option C is correct because S3 Glacier Vault Lock enforces a write-once, read-many (WORM) model for archived documents. It lets you deploy and lock a vault policy that cannot be changed once locked, preventing future modification or deletion of archived data for a defined retention period, which satisfies strict legal and compliance obligations for immutable records.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Versioning keeps multiple versions of objects but does not enforce immutability, since versions can still be deleted. Option B is incorrect because an S3 bucket policy controls access permissions and can be altered by authorized users, providing no WORM guarantee. Option D is incorrect because MFA delete adds a requirement for multi-factor authentication before deleting object versions, but it does not enforce a compliance-grade, unchangeable WORM retention model the way Glacier Vault Lock does.

407.text:
Options B and D are correct because they reflect core benefits of the AWS Cloud. Increased speed for trying out new projects comes from on-demand, self-service provisioning that lets teams experiment without procuring hardware, lowering the cost of failure. Flexibility to scale up in minutes as the application becomes popular reflects elasticity, where resources can be added or removed quickly to match demand rather than waiting weeks to procure capacity.

Option A is incorrect because the cloud replaces large upfront capital expenses with variable, pay-as-you-go operating costs, which is the opposite of this statement. Option C is incorrect because AWS manages and secures the physical infrastructure under the shared responsibility model, so customers do not have complete control over physical security. Option E is incorrect because customers select Regions and Availability Zones but cannot choose the specific physical data centers that host their servers.

408.text:
Option A is correct because deploying Amazon EC2 instances across multiple AWS Regions provides high availability that withstands a natural disaster affecting an entire geographic area. Regions are separated by large geographic distances, so a disaster in one Region does not impact instances running in another, allowing the application to remain available even during a regional-scale event.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront locations are edge caches for content delivery and do not run EC2 instances. Option C is incorrect because edge locations are points of presence for caching and routing, not places where EC2 instances are launched for compute redundancy. Option D is incorrect because AWS Local Zones place compute closer to end users within a metropolitan area but are tied to a parent Region, so they do not provide the geographic separation needed to survive a regional natural disaster.

409.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon SageMaker is the fully managed AWS service purpose-built to build, train, and deploy machine learning models end to end. It provides hosted notebooks for development, managed training infrastructure with automatic scaling, built-in algorithms, and one-click deployment to managed inference endpoints, covering the entire ML lifecycle that the company requires.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Personalize is a high-level AI service for delivering individualized recommendations; it does not let you build and train arbitrary custom models. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Comprehend is a managed natural language processing service that extracts insights from text, not a platform for building and deploying your own models. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Forecast is a specialized service for time-series forecasting only and does not provide the general-purpose build, train, and deploy workflow that SageMaker offers.

410.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) is designed for data that is accessed less frequently but still requires rapid, millisecond access when needed. It offers a lower per-gigabyte storage price than S3 Standard while delivering the same low-latency, immediate retrieval, making it the most cost-effective choice for rarely accessed audio files that must be retrieved instantly.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Standard carries higher storage costs and is optimized for frequently accessed data, so it is not the most cost-effective for rarely accessed files. Option C is incorrect because S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval is an archival class with retrieval times ranging from minutes to hours, which violates the immediate-retrieval requirement. Option D is incorrect because S3 Glacier Deep Archive is the lowest-cost archival tier with retrieval times of up to 12 hours, making immediate access impossible despite its low storage price.

411.text:
Options A and D are correct because under the AWS shared responsibility model, AWS is responsible for the security "of" the cloud, which includes the underlying network infrastructure and the virtualization layer (the hypervisor) that runs customer workloads, as well as the physical security of the hardware and data center facilities. AWS manages and controls these foundational components so customers never have to.

Option B is incorrect because security of application data is the customer's responsibility; the customer controls and protects their own content in the cloud. Option C is incorrect because guest operating systems, including patching and configuration, are managed by the customer, not AWS. Option E is incorrect because credentials and policies, such as IAM users, access keys, and permissions, fall under customer-managed identity and access management. These all represent security "in" the cloud, which the model assigns to the customer.
